title: Workbook — The Sources of Prayer
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Section 1: Remembering the Essentials

Answer each question in your own words. Consult the lesson if you need to.

  1. What are the four principal sources of Christian prayer identified in the lesson and the Catechism (CCC 2650-2696)?
  2. What is Lectio Divina? Name and briefly describe its four steps.
  3. Why is the Eucharist called «the source and summit of the Christian life, » and what does this mean for our prayer?
  4. What do the theological virtues — faith, hope, and charity — have to do with prayer?
  5. What does it mean to «pray without ceasing» (1 Thess 5:17), and how is this possible in a busy life?
  6. Who were the Desert Fathers, and what was their contribution to the tradition of prayer?

Section 2: For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly and at length. This is for your eyes, not for grading.

1. Describe your current relationship with the Bible. Do you read it regularly? If not, what obstacles stand in the way? If yes, how do you approach it — as a textbook, as literature, or as the living Word of God?

Be honest. There is no right or wrong answer here, only what is true for you. Naming the obstacle is the first step toward overcoming it.

 

 

 

 

2. Read Psalm 51 slowly. David prays this psalm after committing adultery and murder — not sins most of us have committed, but the posture of total honesty before God applies to every sin. Write down a verse or phrase that resonates with you. Why did it strike you?

God does not want polished prayers; he wants honest ones. What would it feel like to bring your real self — failures and all — to prayer?

 

 

 

 

3. How do you participate in the Mass? Are you actively engaged, or do you attend more out of habit and obligation? If the latter troubles you, do not be ashamed: the Mass is deep, and many of us need help learning to enter it. What is one thing you could do to participate more consciously?

Concrete suggestions: arrive five minutes early, read the readings beforehand, follow the Eucharistic Prayer with attention, or choose one phrase from the liturgy to carry with you through the week.

 

 

 

 

4. St. Paul tells us to «pray without ceasing.» Choose one ordinary activity you do every day — making coffee, walking to work, brushing your teeth, waiting for the bus. How could that activity become a prayer? What would change inside you if you began to offer it to God?

Prayer is not a separate compartment of life; it is the soul's breathing. The small offerings prepare the heart for the great ones.

 

 

 

 

5. The lesson presents Mary as the model of prayer. Read her Magnificat (Lk 1:46-55) aloud. What does her prayer reveal about her relationship with God? What does it mean to «magnify the Lord» in your own life and circumstances?

Mary does not magnify herself; she magnifies God. Is your prayer more about yourself or more about God? There is no judgment in the question — only an invitation.

 

 

 

 

6. Of all the sources of prayer presented in this lesson — Scripture, Liturgy, theological virtues, daily events, Mary, the saints — which one do you sense God asking you to cultivate most over the coming week? Make a concrete, specific plan.

Not «I will pray more.» Try: «I will read five verses of the Gospel every morning before checking my phone» or «I will pray one decade of the Rosary on my commute.» Small and concrete beats large and vague.

 

 

 

 

Section 3: Practical Activities

Complete at least two of these activities this week.

Activity A: Practice Lectio Divina with a Gospel Passage

Choose one of these passages: John 15:1-8 (the Vine and the Branches), Luke 5:1-11 (the Call of Peter), or Mark 10:46-52 (Bartimaeus). Follow the four steps of Lectio Divina:

  • Lectio (Read): Read the passage aloud, slowly. Read it twice. Which word or phrase catches your attention?
  • Meditatio (Meditate): Sit with that word or phrase. Roll it around in your mind. What is God saying to you through it, right now, in your life?
  • Oratio (Pray): Speak to God from your heart. Respond to what you have heard. Give thanks, ask, confess, surrender — whatever the Word stirs in you.
  • Contemplatio (Contemplate): Be still. Rest in God's presence. No words needed. Stay there for at least two minutes.

Afterward, write down what you discovered. What was the word or phrase? What happened in your heart?

 

 

 

 

 

Activity B: Pray with the Psalms for One Week

Choose seven psalms, one for each day. Use this selection or make your own:

DayPsalmTheme
1Psalm 1The Two Ways
2Psalm 23The Good Shepherd
3Psalm 27Trust in Danger
4Psalm 51Repentance
5Psalm 63Thirst for God
6Psalm 103Bless the Lord
7Psalm 150Universal Praise

Read each day's psalm aloud. Mark the verse or phrase that speaks to you most. At the end of the week, write a brief reflection on the experience.

 

 

 

Activity C: Participate Consciously in the Mass

At your next Sunday Mass:

  1. Arrive five minutes early. Sit in silence. Ask the Holy Spirit to open your heart.
  2. Read the readings beforehand (they are available online at the USCCB website or in any Catholic app).
  3. During the Eucharistic Prayer, pay particular attention to the words of consecration.
  4. After receiving Communion, do not immediately begin the post-Communion hymn in your head. Sit with the Lord who has just entered you. Speak to him, or simply be with him, for as long as the silence allows.
  5. After Mass, write down one phrase from the readings, the homily, or the prayers that you want to carry with you through the week.

 

 

 

Activity D: Try the Jesus Prayer for One Day

The Desert Fathers prayed: «Lord Jesus Christ, Son of God, have mercy on me, a sinner.» Try repeating this prayer quietly throughout one day. Say it while you wait in line, while you walk, while you work. Let it become the background music of your day. In the evening, write down what you noticed.

 

 

 

Activity E: Pray One Decade of the Rosary

Choose one mystery — joyful, luminous, sorrowful, or glorious. Look up the Scripture passage for that mystery. Read it. Then pray the decade slowly: one Our Father, ten Hail Marys, one Glory Be. While you pray the Hail Marys, keep the scene of the mystery in your mind. Afterward, write one insight from the experience.

 

 

 

Activity F: Practice the Daily Examen

At the end of each day this week, take five minutes for the Ignatian Examen:

  1. Become aware of God's presence. Ask for light to see the day as he sees it.
  2. Review the day with gratitude. What were its gifts?
  3. Review the day with honesty. Where did you respond well to God's grace? Where did you resist it?
  4. Speak to God about what you have seen. Thank him. Ask forgiveness. Seek his help for tomorrow.

 

 

 

Section 4: Memory Verses

Memorize one or both of these passages this week.

«Your word is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path.»

— Psalm 119:105

«Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly; teach and admonish one another in all wisdom; and with gratitude in your hearts sing psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs to God.»

— Colossians 3:16

Section 5: Prayer for This Week

Lord Jesus Christ,
you are the living Word of the Father,
and you have given us streams of living water
from which to drink.

Open my heart to your holy Scriptures;
let me not read them as dead letters
but hear them as your living voice.

Draw me deeply into the sacred Liturgy,
that in every Eucharist I may be caught up
into your own prayer to the Father.

Strengthen in me the virtues of faith, hope, and love,
so that even when I feel nothing
my prayer may rest on your gift and not on my strength.

Teach me, through the example of the Blessed Virgin Mary,
to magnify you and not myself.

Let the events of this day become my prayer,
and let my prayer become my life,
until the day when prayer is no longer needed
because I see you face to face.
Amen.

Section 6: This Week's Resolutions

Write one resolution for the week ahead. Make it small, concrete, and achievable.

This week, I will...

 

 

 

 

Print Workbook
